title: Third Party Document Trap
query: The bank refused my documents saying the commercial invoice was issued by a third party, not the beneficiary. But I used my trading company to issue it — I AM the beneficiary. The trading company and the beneficiary are the same entity, just different letterhead. Is the bank right?
answer:
The bank's refusal is technically valid — but whether it's *correct* depends on one key fact: does the name on the invoice letterhead match the beneficiary name in the LC?

Under UCP600 Article 18, the invoice must *appear* to have been issued by the beneficiary. The operative word is "appear" — the bank examines documents on their face. If your trading company's letterhead shows a different name than what's in the LC as the beneficiary, the bank sees a document issued by a stranger to the credit, and that's a legitimate discrepancy [UCP600-18A, CROSSDOC-INV-LC-4].

The fact that you *know* they're the same entity doesn't help you at the counter. Banks don't look behind the documents [UCP600-18-DERIVED-001].

That said, if the names are genuinely the same — just styled differently (e.g., "ABC Trading Co. Ltd" vs "ABC Trading Company Limited") — that's a different argument. The standard is whether the invoice *appears* to be issued by the beneficiary, and minor stylistic differences in a name are generally not treated as a discrepancy [DOCDEX-010].

What to do now:
- Compare the exact name on the invoice letterhead against the exact beneficiary name in Field 59 of the LC.
- If they differ in substance (not just punctuation or abbreviation), the bank is right and you need a corrected invoice.
- If they're the same name in different formatting, push back in writing citing the "appear to be" standard and request the bank reconsider.

What still depends on your transaction: The exact wording of both names, and whether this LC has been transferred (which would change the issuer requirement entirely).

A commercial invoice must appear to have been issued by the beneficiary named in the LC. An invoice issued by any other
Invoice must be issued by the LC beneficiary (or name appearing to be the same).

title: Tolerance on LC Amount
query: My LC amount is USD 50,000. I shipped goods worth USD 52,300. The LC doesn't say anything about tolerance. Will the bank accept my documents or is this a discrepancy?
answer:
This is a discrepancy. Your drawing amount of USD 52,300 exceeds the LC amount of USD 50,000 by USD 2,300 — that's 4.6% over, and the bank cannot pay more than the LC amount regardless of what you shipped.

Here's why the tolerance rules don't save you here:

- The ±5% quantity tolerance [UCP600 Art 30(b)] only applies when the LC doesn't stipulate a specific quantity in packing units or individual items — and critically, it only works if the total drawing still doesn't exceed the LC amount. Your drawing exceeds the LC amount, so this tolerance doesn't help.
- The 5% under-drawing tolerance [UCP600 Art 30(c)] allows you to draw *less* than the LC amount when full quantity is shipped. That's the opposite of your situation.
- The ±10% tolerance [UCP600 Art 30(a)] only applies when the LC uses the words "about" or "approximately." Yours doesn't.

What you should do: You have two practical options. First, amend your commercial invoice to draw exactly USD 50,000 — the maximum the LC permits — and absorb or separately recover the USD 2,300 difference outside the LC (direct payment, open account, etc.). Second, request an LC amendment from the applicant to increase the LC amount to at least USD 52,300 before presenting documents. Don't present at USD 52,300 as-is — the bank will refuse it.

citations.excerpt: LC amount tolerance of 5% applies unless LC prohibits or states a specific quantity without tolerance.

title: Late Presentation vs Expired LC
query: I shipped on March 1. My LC expires April 30. I'm presenting documents on March 25 — that's 24 days after shipment. The LC doesn't mention any specific presentation period. Is my presentation late?
answer:
Yes, your presentation is late — and this is a discrepancy.

The rule is clear: documents must be presented no later than 21 calendar days after the date of shipment, and no later than the LC expiry date. Both conditions must be met [UCP600 Art. 14(c)]. Your LC doesn't specify a different presentation period, so the default 21-day rule applies. March 1 + 21 days = March 22. You're presenting on March 25 — three days late. The fact that your LC doesn't expire until April 30 doesn't help you here. Being within expiry is necessary but not sufficient [UCP600 Art. 14(c); DOCDEX-001].

What this means practically: The bank is entitled to refuse your documents on the basis of late presentation. This is a hard discrepancy, not a grey area.

Your options:

- Ask the bank to accept the documents on an approval basis (they contact the applicant and seek a waiver). The applicant can waive the discrepancy, but they're not obligated to.
- If the applicant agrees, get that waiver confirmed quickly — you still have time before the April 30 expiry.
- If the applicant refuses, you're outside the LC and would need to fall back on your underlying sales contract.

Don't delay. Contact your bank today and flag the late presentation proactively — it's better than waiting for a formal refusal notice.

citations.excerpt: Documents must be presented within 21 days of shipment (or LC-specified period) and before expiry.

title: Multimodal vs Bill of Lading
query: My client's LC calls for a 'Bill of Lading' but the shipment is container door-to-door from Jebel Ali to Rotterdam, with a truck leg from the factory to the port. The shipping line will only issue a multimodal transport document, not a marine bill of lading. Can I present the multimodal document against an LC that says 'Bill of Lading'?
answer:
The Rotterdam Rules (UN Convention on Contracts for the International Carriage of Goods Wholly or Partly by Sea, 2009) extend the carrier's liability period beyond the sea leg to the full door-to-door transport movement: from the point where the carrier receives the goods from the shipper to the point of delivery to the consignee. [unknown n/a]

- [unknown n/a] Commodity trade often uses Contracts of Affreightment (COA) or consecutive voyage charters for bulk shipments over a period (grain, coal, iron ore, fertiliser).

- [Incoterms 2020 n/a] The transport document presented under an LC must be compatible with both the Incoterm and the mode of transport.

title: RCEP Cumulation
query: I manufacture car brake pads in Thailand using steel imported from Japan and rubber from Malaysia. I want to export to South Korea under RCEP preferential tariff. Can I cumulate the Japanese and Malaysian inputs to meet the origin requirement?
answer:
Yes, RCEP's full cumulation rule is designed exactly for this kind of multi-country supply chain. Under [RCEP Chapter 3, Article 3.4], production processes carried out in any RCEP member country can be counted toward origin determination — so your Japanese steel and Malaysian rubber can both be cumulated with your Thai manufacturing when assessing whether the brake pads qualify as RCEP originating.

The general origin framework [RCEP Chapter 3, Article 3.2] gives you three pathways: wholly obtained, produced exclusively from originating materials, or satisfying the Product Specific Rules (PSR). Since your inputs are non-originating at the point of import into Thailand, you'll need to check the PSR for brake pads (likely classified under HS 8708.30) to confirm which test applies — typically a tariff classification change, regional value content threshold, or a combination of both.

If you satisfy the PSR with cumulation applied, you'll need a Certificate of Origin (Form RCEP) issued by Thailand's competent authority or via an approved exporter declaration [RCEP Chapter 3, Article 3.16]. Keep your production records and supplier origin documentation — South Korea can request verification through written inquiry or a verification visit [RCEP Chapter 3, Article 3.24].

One important caveat: The retrieved rules confirm RCEP covers 15 Asia-Pacific countries but do not explicitly list them. I cannot confirm Thailand, Japan, Malaysia, and South Korea are all RCEP members from the rules I have. Please verify all four countries' membership with the RCEP Secretariat or your national trade authority before relying on preferential treatment — getting this wrong means denied tariff preference and potential customs penalties.

What still depends on your transaction: the specific PSR for your brake pad HS code, and whether your Japanese steel and Malaysian rubber suppliers can provide origin declarations to support your cumulation claim.

title: USMCA Automotive Rules of Origin
query: We assemble passenger vehicles in Mexico using engines from the US and transmissions from Japan. What's the Regional Value Content threshold for the finished vehicle under USMCA, and does the Japanese transmission disqualify us?
answer:
The RVC threshold for passenger vehicles under USMCA is 75% on a net cost basis, which has been in effect since 2023. [USMCA Chapter 4, Appendix]

On the Japanese transmission — it doesn't automatically disqualify you, but it does count as non-originating material, which means its value works against your RVC calculation. The key question is whether enough of your total vehicle value comes from North American content (Mexico, US, Canada) to still clear 75%. Your US-sourced engines help significantly here, since USMCA allows accumulation of production and value across all three member countries — so the US engine content counts fully toward your RVC. [USMCA Chapter 4, Article 4.4]

Two additional requirements you need to be aware of beyond RVC:

- Labor Value Content (LVC): A portion of the vehicle's value must come from workers earning at least $16/hour. This is a separate USMCA-specific test. [USMCA Chapter 4, Article 4.18]
- Steel and aluminum: 70% of your steel and aluminum purchases must be sourced from North America. [USMCA Chapter 4]

So the Japanese transmission is a headwind, not a dealbreaker — it depends on the math.

What still depends on your transaction: The actual value of the transmission as a share of total vehicle net cost; whether your LVC calculation clears the required threshold; and your steel/aluminum sourcing breakdown. You'll need a full RVC calculation before you can certify origin with confidence.

citations.excerpt: USMCA uniquely requires Labor Value Content - percentage of vehicle value from high-wage labor ($16+/hour).
USMCA has strict automotive rules. Passenger vehicles require 75% RVC (net cost) by 2023.
Goods qualify as originating if wholly obtained, produced entirely from originating materials, or satisfy PSR using CTC
USMCA allows accumulation of production/value across three parties.
Automotive: 70% of steel/aluminum purchases must be from North America.

title: Bangladesh RCEP Trick Question
query: I export ready-made garments from Bangladesh to Australia. Can I use RCEP certificate of origin to get preferential tariff since Australia is an RCEP member?
answer:
The critical issue here is membership, and I have to be straight with you: the rules I have don't explicitly confirm whether Bangladesh is an RCEP member. Before anything else, you need to verify this with the RCEP Secretariat or the Australian Border Force (ABF) website. Getting this wrong means your CoO gets rejected at customs and you lose the preferential tariff — so don't assume.

If Bangladesh is an RCEP member, here's how the framework applies to your situation:

- You'd need a Form RCEP Certificate of Origin issued by a competent authority or an approved exporter declaration [RCEP Chapter 3, Article 3.16]. In Bangladesh, that's typically the Export Promotion Bureau (EPB) — but confirm this locally.
- Your garments must qualify as RCEP originating — either wholly obtained in Bangladesh, made from originating materials, or meeting the Product Specific Rules (PSR) for your HS code [RCEP Chapter 3, Article 3.2]. For ready-made garments, the PSR is usually a yarn-forward or fabric-forward rule — check the RCEP PSR annex for your specific HS code.
- The CoO is valid for 12 months from issue date [RCEP Chapter 3].
- Australia can request origin verification, so keep your production records [RCEP Chapter 3, Article 3.24].

What still depends on your transaction: Your garment's HS code (determines which PSR applies), whether your fabric/yarn sourcing meets that PSR, and — most importantly — Bangladesh's RCEP membership status.

RCEP Certificate of Origin valid for 12 months from date of issue.

title: UAE Transshipment and Iran Sanctions
query: We received an LC for medical equipment from a German exporter to a company in Dubai. The goods are listed as medical devices but the end-user certificate shows a hospital in Tehran, Iran. The applicant says medical goods are exempt from sanctions. Are they right? What should we check?
answer:
This is a serious red flag — stop and escalate before processing. The applicant's claim that "medical goods are exempt" is a dangerous oversimplification.

The humanitarian exemption is narrow and conditional. Under OFAC's Iran sanctions, there is a general license for humanitarian transactions involving medicine and medical devices (OFAC, 31 CFR 560.530), but it does not authorize transactions that involve the Government of Iran or any SDN, items with potential military end-use, or routing through SWIFT-excluded Iranian banks. A commercial trade transaction cannot simply claim the humanitarian GL — there must be a clear showing of exclusively humanitarian purpose (OFAC-IRAN-006).

Here's what you need to check:

- Diversion risk. The LC applicant is in Dubai but the end-user is in Tehran. UAE banks are required to check shipment routing to ensure goods aren't transiting to sanctioned destinations (AE-ROUTING-001, CBUAE Guidelines). An end-use certificate pointing to Iran makes this exactly the scenario those checks exist for (AE-SANCTIONS-002).
- Dual-use screening. Some medical devices (imaging equipment, certain electronics) can be dual-use. Screen the specific goods description against dual-use control lists (SANC-DUALUSE-001).
- EU export controls. The exporter is German — EU sanctions and export licensing requirements apply on the exporter's side independently of OFAC.

The LC structure here — Dubai applicant, Iranian end-user — is a classic TBML/sanctions evasion pattern. Do not rely on the applicant's assurance. This needs your compliance team and likely external sanctions counsel involved immediately.

What still depends on your transaction: the exact medical device classification, whether any party is an SDN, which Iranian bank (if any) is involved, and whether the German exporter holds an EU export license.

citations.excerpt: UAE banks may require end-use certificates/statements for sensitive goods to prevent diversion.
Check if goods are prohibited for export to Russia under EU sanctions packages.
OFAC GL authorizes certain personal remittances to or from Iran (31 CFR 560.550) and humanitarian transactions involving

title: Russian Oil Price Cap
query: A client wants to ship Russian crude oil from Novorossiysk to India. The price is $58 per barrel. Is this compliant with the G7 price cap? What vessel checks do I need to do?
answer:
At $58/bbl, the price is below the G7 price cap of USD 60/bbl for Russian crude, so on price alone the transaction can be serviced by coalition-country operators — meaning EU/G7 insurers, banks, and shipping companies can provide transport, financing, and insurance [EU Regulation 2022/1904; Council Regulation (EU) 833/2014 Article 3n]. But price compliance is only one piece. Here's what else you need to nail down:

- Attestation requirement. The vessel and your client must provide a written attestation that the cargo was purchased at or below the price cap. This is mandatory for any coalition-country service provider touching the shipment [G7/EU Price Cap Coalition — VESSEL-RUSSIA-OIL-001].

- Shadow fleet screening. Check the vessel carefully. Red flags include: flag of convenience registered after Feb 2022 (Gabon, Palau, Cameroon, Togo, Belize are common), recently formed owner/manager with no verifiable track record, vessel purchased by a new entity in 2022–present, and opaque beneficial ownership. Any of these indicators should trigger escalation [OFAC Shadow Fleet Advisory 2024; EU 14th Sanctions Package — VESSEL-FLAG-003].

- US nexus matters. If any US person, US-dollar clearing, or US-nexus bank is involved, note that OFAC prohibits US persons from financing the import of Russian-origin crude entirely — the price cap doesn't save you on the US side [EO 14024 Directive 4; EO 14066; 31 CFR 587.201 — OFAC-RUSSIA-006].

What still depends on your transaction: Which jurisdiction your bank, insurer, and shipowner sit in (EU/UK/US each have different rules); whether the vessel or any counterparty is on the SDN or EU consolidated sanctions list; and whether the $58 price includes all costs or just FOB — the price cap applies to the purchase price and coalition guidance specifies how ancillary costs factor in, which my retrieved rules don't detail fully. Verify that with your compliance team against the latest coalition FAQ.

citations.reference: EU Regulation 2022/1904; G7 Price Cap Coalition; Council Regulation (EU) 833/2014 Article 3n

title: CIF vs FOB Insurance Under LC
query: My supplier in India is shipping fabric under CIF New York. The LC calls for CIF. Who is responsible for insurance — me or the supplier? And does the insurance document need to be presented with the LC documents? My supplier says insurance is 'included in the price' so I don't need a separate policy.
answer:
Your supplier is responsible for the insurance — that's the whole point of CIF. Under Incoterms 2020 CIF, the seller must procure marine cargo insurance covering the buyer's risk during transit, at a minimum of Institute Cargo Clauses (C) [Incoterms 2020 - CIF]. "Included in the price" just means the cost of the premium is built into the CIF price — it doesn't mean you skip the document.

And yes, the insurance document absolutely must be presented under the LC. Because your LC calls for CIF, the insurance certificate is a mandatory document [UCP600 Art. 28, Incoterms 2020 CIF]. The minimum coverage required is 110% of the CIF invoice value [UCP600 Art. 28 / ISBP745 K5, Incoterms 2020 CIF]. The certificate must also be transferable to you as the buyer [Incoterms 2020 - CIF].

If your supplier presents documents without an insurance certificate, the bank will raise a discrepancy and can refuse to pay. This is not a grey area.

Tell your supplier: the insurance certificate — not just the premium being "included" — must be presented as part of the LC document set, endorsed or made out in a way that transfers the benefit to you.

What still depends on your transaction: Whether your LC's Field 46A explicitly lists the insurance document (it should), and whether it specifies a coverage level above the ICC(C) minimum. Check those LC terms — if the LC requires ICC(A) or a specific percentage above 110%, the supplier must meet that higher standard.

citations.excerpt: Most common LC term. Seller pays freight AND insurance to destination. Risk transfers on board. SEA ONLY. Insurance mini
CIF and CIP Incoterms require insurance. If LC uses these terms, insurance document mandatory.

title: HDFC Bank Presentation Period
query: I received an LC from HDFC Bank India. The LC says 'Documents must be presented within 15 days of shipment.' UCP600 says 21 days. Which one applies? Can HDFC override UCP600?
answer:
The 15 days applies. HDFC hasn't "overridden" UCP600 — they've used a mechanism UCP600 explicitly allows.

UCP600 Article 14(c) [MT700-48-001] sets 21 days as the default presentation period, not a minimum or a fixed rule. The LC itself can specify a shorter (or longer) period, and when it does, that stated period governs. HDFC putting "15 days" in Field 48 of the MT700 is exactly how this is supposed to work. The 21-day default only kicks in when the LC is silent on presentation period.

So no, HDFC isn't overriding UCP600 — they're operating within it.

What this means for you practically:

- Your clock starts on the shipment date (the date on the bill of lading or equivalent transport document)
- You have 15 calendar days from that date to present complying documents to the nominated bank
- You must also stay within the LC's overall expiry date [MT700-31D-001] — whichever deadline falls first controls

15 days is tight. If your shipment is going by sea and your freight forwarder takes a few days to release the BL, you can run out of time fast. Get your document preparation process moving the moment goods are loaded.

What still depends on your transaction: whether the LC specifies banking days or calendar days for the 15-day count. If it doesn't say, treat them as calendar days — that's standard practice.

title: Legal Opinion on DOCDEX
query: The issuing bank refused my documents citing 3 discrepancies. I believe 2 of them are wrong — the bank is misinterpreting UCP600 Article 14. I want to take this to ICC DOCDEX arbitration. Can you tell me if I have a strong case and whether I should proceed?
answer:
That's a frustrating position to be in — let me give you a straight answer.

Whether you have a strong DOCDEX case depends on two things: whether the bank's refusal notice was procedurally valid, and whether their interpretation of Article 14 is actually wrong on the merits.

On the procedural side, check this first. Under [UCP600 Article 16(c)(ii)], the refusal notice must state *each* discrepancy specifically. If the bank listed all three discrepancies clearly and gave notice by the close of the fifth banking day after presentation, the notice is procedurally valid. If it was vague or late, [UCP600 Article 16(f)] kicks in — the bank is precluded from claiming non-compliance entirely, and you may not even need DOCDEX.

On the Article 14 merits. The key standard is that data in documents need not be *identical* but must not *conflict* [UCP600 Article 14]. If the bank is treating a non-conflicting difference as a discrepancy, that's a misapplication of the rule — and that's exactly the kind of dispute DOCDEX handles well.

Before you file, though: DOCDEX gives an expert opinion, not a binding judgment. It carries significant persuasive weight, but enforcement still depends on the issuing bank and the courts in its jurisdiction. It's also not free. Weigh that against the value of the LC.

What still depends on your transaction: the exact wording of the two disputed discrepancies, the specific LC terms, and whether the refusal notice was issued within the five-day window.
